news_summary: Today's health news highlights promising advances in managing long COVID fatigue, heart and brain protection through diet, effective weight maintenance with intermittent fasting, and strategies to mitigate the impact of night shift work on sleep.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité santé d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées prometteuses dans la prise en charge de la fatigue liée au COVID-19 de longue durée, la protection du cœur et du cerveau grâce à l'alimentation, le maintien efficace du poids grâce au jeûne intermittent, ainsi que des stratégies visant à atténuer l'impact du travail de nuit sur le sommeil.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de salud de hoy destacan avances prometedores en el tratamiento de la fatiga asociada al «COVID prolongado», la protección del corazón y el cerebro a través de la alimentación, el mantenimiento eficaz del peso mediante el ayuno intermitente y estrategias para mitigar el impacto del trabajo en turnos de noche sobre el sueño.

articles.title: Common antidepressant may ease long COVID’s crushing fatigue
articles.summary: A low-cost antidepressant may offer new hope for people struggling with long COVID fatigue. In a randomized clinical trial involving 399 adults, fluvoxamine significantly reduced fatigue and improved quality of life compared with a placebo, making it one of the first medications to show meaningful benefits for this disabling condition.
articles.content:
A widely available antidepressant may provide meaningful relief for people experiencing persistent fatigue from long COVID, according to a global clinical trial co-led by McMaster University.

Researchers found that fluvoxamine (sold under the brand name Luvox), an inexpensive medication already commonly used to treat depression and other conditions, reduced fatigue and improved quality of life in adults with long COVID. The randomized, placebo-controlled trial was published in the Annals of Internal Medicine.

A Potential Treatment for Long COVID Fatigue

Fatigue is among the most frequent and disabling symptoms reported by people with long COVID. For some, the exhaustion is severe enough to interfere with employment, family responsibilities, and everyday activities. Despite the scale of the problem, there are still few treatments supported by strong clinical evidence.

"This is an important step forward for patients who have been desperate for evidence-based options," says Edward Mills, senior author, professor in McMaster's Department of Health Research Methods, Evidence, and Impact, and co-principal investigator of the trial. "Fluvoxamine showed consistent and meaningful benefits, and because it's already widely used and well understood, it has clear potential for clinical use."

Researchers from Canada, Brazil, and the United States jointly led the study. Clinical sites were located in Belo Horizonte and throughout Minas Gerais, Brazil.

The REVIVE-TOGETHER trial brought together investigators from McMaster University, the University of British Columbia, Stanford University, the University of Pittsburgh, Duke University, Georgetown University, and several Brazilian institutions.

Testing Fluvoxamine and Metformin

The trial included 399 adults in Brazil who had experienced ongoing fatigue for at least 90 days after a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection. Participants were randomly placed into one of three groups and received fluvoxamine (sold under the brand name Luvox), metformin (a common diabetes medication), or a placebo for 60 days.

"We wanted to test whether two existing, widely available, and affordable medications could help. Both had biological reasons to think they might work against long COVID fatigue, but neither had been rigorously tested for this purpose in a proper clinical trial," says Mills.

Fluvoxamine performed better than the placebo in reducing fatigue. The statistical analysis indicated a 99 percent probability that the medication was more effective than the placebo. Participants who received fluvoxamine also reported gains in overall quality of life across several measures.

Metformin did not produce the same results. Earlier research found that taking metformin during the initial stage of a COVID infection could lower the risk of later developing long COVID. In this trial, however, the drug provided no meaningful improvement for people who already had established long COVID fatigue.

An Adaptive Clinical Trial Design

Researchers used a Bayesian adaptive trial design, which allowed them to end individual treatment groups early once the evidence became sufficiently clear. This approach can produce reliable conclusions more quickly than a conventional trial while maintaining scientific rigor.

"The trial used a sophisticated adaptive design that allowed it to reach conclusions more efficiently than traditional trials, stopping early when the evidence was clear enough - a design innovation as important as the findings themselves," says Gilmar Reis, lead author, researcher with Cardresearch, a Brazilian clinical research center based in Belo Horizonte. Reis is also a part-time associate professor at McMaster.

More Research Is Still Needed

Long COVID continues to pose a major global health challenge and is estimated to affect about 65 million people worldwide. Because proven therapies remain scarce, most medical recommendations focus on supportive strategies, including activity pacing and management of individual symptoms.

The researchers caution that fluvoxamine is not a complete solution for long COVID. The condition can involve many different symptoms and biological processes, and the medication appears specifically promising for fatigue management.

Additional studies will be needed to determine which patients are most likely to benefit, understand why the drug works, and explore whether it could be used alongside other developing treatments.

"This trial gives clinicians their first strong evidence for a medication that helps reduce long COVID fatigue. Patients want something they can try today - and this finding brings us closer to that reality," says Jamie Forrest, corresponding author and postdoctoral research fellow at the University of British Columbia.

The research was funded by The Latona Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant clinical trial demonstrating that a widely available, low-cost antidepressant (fluvoxamine) can meaningfully reduce fatigue and improve quality of life for people suffering from long COVID, a major global health issue affecting millions. The study is well-detailed, involves multiple international research institutions, and presents a promising treatment option with broad public health implications.
articles.personality_title: Common antidepressant fluvoxamine eases fatigue in long COVID patients
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people who recover from COVID-19 continue to feel very tired for months, a condition called long COVID fatigue. Until now, there have been few proven treatments to help with this ongoing exhaustion.

**What happened** – A large clinical trial tested two existing medicines, fluvoxamine (an antidepressant) and metformin (a diabetes drug), on 399 adults in Brazil who had long COVID fatigue for at least 90 days. The study found that fluvoxamine significantly reduced fatigue and improved quality of life compared to a placebo. Metformin did not show the same benefits.

**Impact** – This is one of the first strong scientific studies to show that a low-cost, widely available medicine can help people struggling with long COVID fatigue. Because fluvoxamine is already used for other health issues and is well understood, doctors might be able to start using it more quickly to help patients.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to learn more about who benefits most from fluvoxamine and why it works. They also plan to study how it can be combined with other treatments for long COVID. More research will help doctors better treat this complex condition.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Fluvoxamine, a common antidepressant, has been proven in a clinical trial to reduce fatigue and improve life quality for people with long COVID.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un antidépresseur courant réduit la fatigue liée au COVID long
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses personnes ressentent une fatigue intense pendant des mois après avoir eu la COVID-19, ce qu'on appelle la fatigue du COVID long. Jusqu'à présent, il y avait peu de traitements prouvés pour soulager cette fatigue persistante.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un grand essai clinique a testé deux médicaments existants, la fluvoxamine (un antidépresseur) et la metformine (un médicament contre le diabète), sur 399 adultes au Brésil souffrant de fatigue liée au COVID long depuis au moins 90 jours. L'étude a montré que la fluvoxamine réduisait significativement la fatigue et améliorait la qualité de vie par rapport à un placebo. La metformine n'a pas montré les mêmes effets.

**Impact** – C’est l’une des premières études solides à démontrer qu’un médicament peu coûteux et largement disponible peut aider les personnes souffrant de fatigue liée au COVID long. Comme la fluvoxamine est déjà utilisée pour d’autres problèmes de santé et bien connue, les médecins pourraient rapidement l’utiliser pour traiter ces patients.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent mieux comprendre qui bénéficie le plus de la fluvoxamine et pourquoi elle fonctionne. Ils prévoient aussi d’étudier son utilisation combinée avec d’autres traitements du COVID long. Davantage de recherches permettront d’améliorer la prise en charge de cette maladie complexe.

**En une phrase** – La fluvoxamine, un antidépresseur courant, a prouvé dans un essai clinique qu’elle réduit la fatigue et améliore la qualité de vie des personnes atteintes de COVID long.
articles.personality_title_es: Antidepresivo común reduce la fatiga en pacientes con COVID prolongado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas que se recuperan de COVID-19 siguen sintiéndose muy cansadas durante meses, una condición llamada fatiga por COVID prolongado. Hasta ahora, había pocos tratamientos probados para ayudar con este cansancio persistente.

**Qué pasó** – Un gran ensayo clínico probó dos medicamentos existentes, fluvoxamina (un antidepresivo) y metformina (un medicamento para la diabetes), en 399 adultos en Brasil que tenían fatiga por COVID prolongado desde hace al menos 90 días. El estudio encontró que la fluvoxamina redujo significativamente la fatiga y mejoró la calidad de vida en comparación con un placebo. La metformina no mostró los mismos beneficios.

**Impacto** – Este es uno de los primeros estudios científicos fuertes que demuestra que un medicamento de bajo costo y fácil acceso puede ayudar a las personas que luchan con la fatiga del COVID prolongado. Como la fluvoxamina ya se usa para otros problemas de salud y es bien conocida, los médicos podrían comenzar a usarla pronto para ayudar a los pacientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren aprender más sobre quién se beneficia más de la fluvoxamina y por qué funciona. También planean estudiar cómo se puede combinar con otros tratamientos para el COVID prolongado. Más investigaciones ayudarán a los médicos a tratar mejor esta condición compleja.

**Una frase para recordar** – La fluvoxamina, un antidepresivo común, ha demostrado en un ensayo clínico que reduce la fatiga y mejora la calidad de vida en personas con COVID prolongado.
articles.image_prompt: A gentle, detailed painting of a glowing, open pill capsule releasing soft, warm light that transforms into a serene, flowing river of energy lifting a heavy, weighted cloud symbolizing fatigue, set against a calm, muted background with natural, simple earth tones.

articles.title: Mediterranean diet may activate tiny proteins that protect the heart and brain
articles.summary: The Mediterranean diet may influence aging through tiny proteins produced inside the cell’s mitochondria. Older adults who followed the diet most closely had higher levels of humanin and SHMOOSE, which have been linked to heart and brain protection. Olive oil, fish, legumes, and fewer refined carbohydrates appeared especially important. The findings could eventually help scientists develop more personalized nutrition plans for healthy aging.
articles.content:
A Mediterranean-style diet may influence aging through an unexpected biological pathway involving tiny proteins produced inside mitochondria, according to a new study led by researchers at the USC Leonard Davis School of Gerontology.

Mitochondria are best known as the structures that generate energy inside cells, but scientists increasingly recognize that they also release chemical signals that affect metabolism, inflammation, stress responses, and aging.

The researchers found that older adults who followed a Mediterranean diet most closely had higher blood levels of two mitochondrial microproteins called humanin and SHMOOSE. Both have previously been associated with protection against cardiovascular disease and neurodegeneration, the gradual loss of nerve cell function seen in conditions such as Alzheimer's disease.

The study was led by Roberto Vicinanza, an instructional associate professor of gerontology at the USC Leonard Davis School.

"These microproteins may act as molecular messengers that translate what we eat into how our cells function and age," Vicinanza said. "It's a new biological pathway that helps explain why the Mediterranean diet is so powerful."

How the Mediterranean Diet May Influence Aging

The Mediterranean diet emphasizes olive oil, fish, legumes, fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and other minimally processed foods. It generally limits refined carbohydrates, heavily processed products, and foods high in added sugar.

Decades of research have linked this eating pattern to a lower risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, cognitive decline, and premature death. However, scientists are still working to identify the cellular processes that may produce those benefits.

For the new study, the research team examined blood samples from older adults who followed the Mediterranean diet to different degrees. Participants with the strongest adherence had significantly higher concentrations of humanin and SHMOOSE.

They also had lower indicators of oxidative stress, a form of cellular damage caused when unstable molecules known as reactive oxygen species overwhelm the body's protective systems. Persistent oxidative stress can injure proteins, fats, and DNA and has been associated with aging and many chronic diseases.

Olive Oil, Fish, and Legumes Showed Strong Links

The findings suggest that individual parts of the Mediterranean diet may contribute differently to mitochondrial health.

Higher consumption of olive oil, fish, and legumes was associated with increased levels of humanin. Olive oil and a lower intake of refined carbohydrates were linked to higher levels of SHMOOSE.

Refined carbohydrates include foods such as white bread, pastries, and many sugary products that have been stripped of much of their natural fiber and nutrients. They are digested quickly and can produce sharp increases in blood sugar.

"These findings suggest that specific components of the Mediterranean diet may directly influence mitochondrial biology," said USC Leonard Davis School Dean and USC Distinguished Professor Pinchas Cohen, the study's senior author. "Humanin and SHMOOSE could serve as biomarkers for adherence to the Mediterranean diet and have clinical significance."

A biomarker is a measurable biological sign that can provide information about health, disease, or how the body is responding to a behavior or treatment. In this case, the two microproteins could potentially help researchers measure how strongly a person's body is responding to a Mediterranean eating pattern.

Tiny Proteins Hidden in Mitochondrial DNA

The study builds on more than 20 years of research led by Cohen, who helped pioneer the discovery of peptides produced by mitochondria.

Most familiar human proteins are made from instructions contained in the DNA inside the cell nucleus. Mitochondria, however, carry a small amount of their own genetic material, inherited separately from nuclear DNA.

Humanin and SHMOOSE come from short sections of the mitochondrial genome known as small open reading frames. These regions were once thought to have little or no function, but researchers now know that some of them produce biologically active microproteins.

Humanin is among the most extensively studied of these molecules. Cohen and his colleagues first identified it in 2003. Subsequent research has connected it to improved insulin sensitivity, cardiovascular protection, longer life span, and the preservation of cognitive function.

Cohen's laboratory later discovered SHMOOSE (Small Human Mitochondrial ORF Over SErine tRNA), which has been linked to brain health. One genetic variant of SHMOOSE has been associated with a greater risk of Alzheimer's disease, while the normal form appears to help shield neurons from damage related to amyloid.

Amyloid is a protein that can accumulate abnormally in the brain and form plaques, one of the major biological features associated with Alzheimer's disease.

"These peptides are emerging as key regulators of aging biology," Cohen said. "They connect mitochondrial function to diseases like Alzheimer's and heart disease and now, potentially, to nutrition."

A Possible New Form of Heart Protection

The researchers also identified a possible connection between humanin and Nox2, an enzyme involved in producing reactive oxygen species.

Although reactive oxygen species play useful roles in normal cell signaling and immune defenses, excessive production can damage tissues and increase oxidative stress.

Higher humanin levels were associated with lower Nox2 activity. This relationship suggests that humanin could help limit oxidative damage and provide added protection for the heart and blood vessels.

The researchers propose that the Mediterranean diet may protect the cardiovascular system in two complementary ways. It may directly reduce oxidative stress while also increasing mitochondrial microproteins that help restrain damaging cellular pathways.

"This could represent a new cardioprotective mechanism of the Mediterranean diet," Vicinanza said.

Taking the Mediterranean Diet to the Global Stage

Vicinanza has also worked to promote the Mediterranean diet beyond the laboratory, presenting it as a model not only for health, but also for culture and environmental sustainability.

He has collaborated with the Municipality of Pollica in Italy, a UNESCO Mediterranean Diet emblematic community, to support the creation of the International Day of the Mediterranean Diet at the United Nations.

The observance will take place each year on November 16. Its goal is to increase awareness of the Mediterranean diet's health, cultural, and environmental importance around the world.

Vicinanza said that mission reflects the broader implications of the new findings.

"We're connecting centuries-old dietary traditions with cutting-edge molecular biology," Vicinanza said. "It supports the idea that healthy eating patterns with little to no ultra-processed foods reflect how humans have eaten over long periods and may create conditions to which mitochondria -- ancient cellular organelles -- are likely adapted."

Cellular organelles are specialized structures that perform specific jobs inside cells. Mitochondria are considered ancient because scientists believe they evolved from bacteria that entered into a cooperative relationship with early cells more than a billion years ago.

A Step Toward Precision Nutrition

The study was relatively small and observational. That means it identified associations between diet and microprotein levels but could not prove that following the Mediterranean diet directly caused those biological changes.

Other factors, including physical activity, overall health, medication use, genetics, and lifestyle, could also have influenced the findings.

Even with those limitations, the results point toward a developing field known as precision nutrition. This approach aims to use biological measurements, genetics, metabolism, and other personal characteristics to design dietary recommendations that are more individualized than standard nutrition advice.

Humanin, SHMOOSE, and related mitochondrial microproteins could eventually help scientists determine which dietary patterns are producing beneficial cellular effects in a particular person.

Future studies will test whether changing a person's diet can directly raise levels of these peptides and whether those increases result in a measurable reduction in disease risk.

"Our goal is to move from observing associations to understanding causality," Vicinanza said. "If we can harness these pathways, we may be able to design nutritional strategies that promote healthy aging at the molecular level."

About the Study

The study, "Mediterranean diet adherence is associated with mitochondrial microproteins Humanin and SHMOOSE; potential role of the Humanin-Nox2 interaction in cardioprotection," was published March 9, 2026, in Frontiers in Nutrition. Coauthors included Junxiang Wan and Kelvin Yen of the USC Leonard Davis School and Vittoria Cammisotto, Francesco Violi and Pasquale Pignatelli of Sapienza University of Rome.

The work was funded by the USC Daryl and Irwin Simon Nutrition for Alzheimer's Disease Prevention Research Fund (to Vicinanza), the Hanson-Thorell Family Research Award (to Vicinanza), National Institutes of Health grant P30AG094848 (to Cohen) and PRIN 2022 grant 000031_23_PP_PIGNATELLI_PRIN_2022-B53D23021240006 (to Pignatelli).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking the Mediterranean diet to the activation of mitochondrial microproteins that protect heart and brain health, offering a new biological pathway that could lead to personalized nutrition strategies for healthy aging. The findings have broad implications for public health, aging populations, and disease prevention, supported by detailed research and potential for future clinical applications.
articles.personality_title: Mediterranean diet linked to tiny proteins that protect heart and brain health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long known that the Mediterranean diet is good for the heart and brain. This diet includes foods like olive oil, fish, legumes, fruits, and vegetables, while limiting refined carbohydrates like white bread and sugary snacks. Researchers wanted to understand how this diet helps protect the body at a tiny, cellular level.

**What happened** – A new study led by researchers at the University of Southern California found that older adults who closely followed the Mediterranean diet had higher levels of two small proteins called humanin and SHMOOSE in their blood. These proteins are made inside mitochondria, the parts of cells that produce energy. The study showed that these proteins are connected to protecting the heart and brain from damage. The researchers also found that eating more olive oil, fish, and legumes was linked to higher levels of these protective proteins.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it reveals a new biological way the Mediterranean diet may help people age healthier. The tiny proteins humanin and SHMOOSE act like messengers inside cells, helping reduce damage caused by harmful molecules. This could explain why people who eat this diet have lower risks of heart disease and brain problems like Alzheimer’s disease. It also shows that certain foods in the diet play a special role in keeping mitochondria healthy.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to find out if changing a person’s diet can directly increase these protective proteins and reduce disease risks. This research could lead to personalized nutrition plans that use these proteins as markers to see how well someone’s diet is working at a cellular level. Future studies will test these ideas to better understand how diet affects aging and health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Following the Mediterranean diet may boost tiny proteins made by mitochondria that help protect the heart and brain, opening new paths for healthy aging through personalized nutrition.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le régime méditerranéen stimule de petites protéines qui protègent le cœur et le cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – On sait depuis longtemps que le régime méditerranéen est bénéfique pour le cœur et le cerveau. Ce régime comprend des aliments comme l'huile d'olive, le poisson, les légumineuses, les fruits et les légumes, tout en limitant les glucides raffinés comme le pain blanc et les sucreries. Les chercheurs ont voulu comprendre comment ce régime protège le corps au niveau cellulaire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude menée par des chercheurs de l'Université de Californie du Sud a montré que les personnes âgées qui suivent de près le régime méditerranéen ont des niveaux plus élevés de deux petites protéines appelées humanine et SHMOOSE dans leur sang. Ces protéines sont fabriquées à l'intérieur des mitochondries, les parties des cellules qui produisent de l'énergie. L'étude a montré que ces protéines protègent le cœur et le cerveau contre les dommages. Manger plus d'huile d'olive, de poisson et de légumineuses était lié à des niveaux plus élevés de ces protéines protectrices.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle révèle une nouvelle façon biologique dont le régime méditerranéen peut aider à vieillir en meilleure santé. Les petites protéines humanine et SHMOOSE agissent comme des messagers dans les cellules, aidant à réduire les dommages causés par des molécules nocives. Cela pourrait expliquer pourquoi les personnes qui suivent ce régime ont un risque plus faible de maladies cardiaques et de problèmes cérébraux comme la maladie d'Alzheimer. Cela montre aussi que certains aliments jouent un rôle spécial pour garder les mitochondries en bonne santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent savoir si changer l'alimentation d'une personne peut augmenter directement ces protéines protectrices et réduire les risques de maladie. Cette recherche pourrait conduire à des plans nutritionnels personnalisés utilisant ces protéines comme indicateurs pour voir l'efficacité du régime au niveau cellulaire. De futures études testeront ces idées pour mieux comprendre comment l'alimentation influence le vieillissement et la santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Suivre le régime méditerranéen pourrait augmenter de petites protéines fabriquées par les mitochondries qui protègent le cœur et le cerveau, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour un vieillissement en santé grâce à la nutrition personnalisée.
articles.personality_title_es: La dieta mediterránea activa pequeñas proteínas que protegen el corazón y el cerebro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Se sabe desde hace tiempo que la dieta mediterránea es buena para el corazón y el cerebro. Esta dieta incluye alimentos como aceite de oliva, pescado, legumbres, frutas y verduras, y limita los carbohidratos refinados como el pan blanco y los dulces. Los científicos querían entender cómo esta dieta protege el cuerpo a nivel celular.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio liderado por investigadores de la Universidad del Sur de California encontró que los adultos mayores que siguieron de cerca la dieta mediterránea tenían niveles más altos de dos pequeñas proteínas llamadas humanina y SHMOOSE en su sangre. Estas proteínas se producen dentro de las mitocondrias, que son las partes de las células que generan energía. El estudio mostró que estas proteínas ayudan a proteger el corazón y el cerebro del daño. Comer más aceite de oliva, pescado y legumbres estaba relacionado con niveles más altos de estas proteínas protectoras.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque revela una nueva forma biológica en que la dieta mediterránea puede ayudar a envejecer con mejor salud. Las pequeñas proteínas humanina y SHMOOSE actúan como mensajeros dentro de las células, ayudando a reducir el daño causado por moléculas dañinas. Esto podría explicar por qué las personas que siguen esta dieta tienen menos riesgo de enfermedades del corazón y problemas cerebrales como el Alzheimer. También muestra que ciertos alimentos tienen un papel especial en mantener saludables las mitocondrias.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren saber si cambiar la dieta de una persona puede aumentar directamente estas proteínas protectoras y reducir el riesgo de enfermedades. Esta investigación podría llevar a planes de nutrición personalizados que usen estas proteínas como señales para ver qué tan bien está funcionando la dieta a nivel celular. Estudios futuros probarán estas ideas para entender mejor cómo la dieta afecta el envejecimiento y la salud.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Seguir la dieta mediterránea puede aumentar pequeñas proteínas producidas por las mitocondrias que protegen el corazón y el cerebro, abriendo nuevas formas para un envejecimiento saludable mediante la nutrición personalizada.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Mediterranean table spread with olive oil, fish, legumes, fruits, and vegetables arranged around a glowing, stylized mitochondrion emitting soft light beams that transform into tiny, shimmering protein shapes labeled by their forms as humanin and SHMOOSE, symbolizing the heart and brain protection these microproteins provide.

articles.title: Intermittent fasting helped people keep weight off for a year
articles.summary: A 12-week intermittent fasting program produced weight-loss benefits that were still visible a year later. Participants who ate within an eight-hour window maintained more weight loss than those who followed their usual longer eating schedule. Both early and late eating windows worked, while early fasting appeared especially helpful for preserving fat loss.
articles.content:
A 12 week intermittent fasting program may help people maintain weight loss for at least a year after the structured intervention ends, according to research involving the University of Granada (UGR).

Published in Clinical Nutrition, the study followed 99 adults who were overweight or obese. Half of the participants were women. Researchers found that limiting daily food intake to an eight hour period helped participants preserve more of their weight loss than maintaining an eating window of 12 hours or longer.

Intermittent Fasting Benefits Persisted for a Year

The researchers examined the popular 16:8 approach to intermittent fasting. Under this method, people fast for 16 hours each day and eat during the remaining eight hours.

The lasting benefits did not depend on whether participants ate earlier or later in the day. An early schedule allowed eating between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m., while a later schedule used a window between 1 p.m. and 9 p.m.

One year after the intervention ended, both the early and late fasting groups had maintained significantly more weight loss than participants who continued eating across 12 hours or more each day. Those assigned to the early schedule also preserved a larger reduction in fat mass.

The findings suggest that time restricted eating may be practical in the short term while also producing effects that continue well beyond the initial program.

Tracking Weight and Body Composition

The study appeared in Clinical Nutrition, the official journal of the European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism. Scientists from the University of Granada (UGR), the Granada Institute for Biomedical Research (ibs.GRANADA), the Public University of Navarra, and the Biomedical Research Networking Center (CIBER) participated in the research.

During the first 12 weeks, all 99 participants received education about following a Mediterranean diet. They were then placed into four groups.

The control group continued using its usual daily eating window of 12 hours or longer. The early fasting group followed an eight hour window beginning before 10:00 a.m. The late fasting group used an eight hour window beginning after 1:00 p.m. Participants in the self selected group chose their own eight hour schedule.

Researchers measured body weight, fat mass, and fat free mass before and after the intervention. They assessed the same measurements again one year after the program had concluded.

The work was part of a broader research project whose primary findings were published in Nature Medicine. Those results showed that participants practicing TRE lost an average of 3-4 kilos more than people who received nutritional guidance alone, regardless of when their eating window occurred.

Weight Changes Remained After the Program

Dr. Alba Camacho Cardeñosa, a researcher at the University Joint Institute for Sport and Health (iMUDS) at the University of Granada (UGR) and a postdoctoral fellow at ibs.GRANADA in the Endocrinology and Nutrition Department at San Cecilio University Clinical Hospital, is the first author of the study.

She explains that "to date, although we knew that intermittent fasting promotes modest weight loss in the short term, it was unclear whether its effects were sustained over time. By evaluating the participants 12 months after the intervention ended, we demonstrated that the changes in body weight persist."

The researchers also point to evidence that the routine may be manageable outside a controlled study. They highlight that "a very positive finding is that one in three people decided to continue practicing intermittent fasting on their own during that year of follow-up, suggesting that it is a relatively easy habit to integrate into daily life."

A Flexible Approach to Weight Management

Researchers from the PROFITH CTS-977 research group at the University of Granada (UGR) led the study through ibs.GRANADA. Professor Jonatan Ruiz Ruiz heads the group.

The project also included collaborators from San Cecilio University Clinical Hospital, Virgen de las Nieves University Hospital in Granada, the Public University of Navarra, the CIBER on Obesity (CIBEROBN), and the CIBER on Frailty and Healthy Aging (CIBERFES).

According to the research team, even a 12 week period of intermittent fasting could provide an effective medium term option for weight management among adults who are overweight or obese.

Because both early and late eating windows produced lasting benefits, patients may be able to choose the schedule that fits their routines most comfortably. That flexibility could make the approach easier to follow and potentially improve its usefulness in obesity treatment.

Research Into Metabolic and Cardiovascular Health

The MP20 group, Biomarkers of Metabolic and Bone Diseases at ibs.GRANADA, studies biological markers and potential treatment targets related to metabolic, bone, and cardiovascular conditions.

The group combines bioinformatics with clinical research to create diagnostic tools and assess possible therapies. Its work includes investigating intermittent fasting as a treatment for obesity and associated health problems, along with studying whether bone markers can help predict cardiovascular risk.

Through its multidisciplinary and collaborative approach, the group aims to produce research with practical clinical applications.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific study demonstrating that a 12-week intermittent fasting program can produce sustained weight loss benefits lasting at least a year, which has broad implications for obesity treatment and public health. The study is well-detailed, involves a meaningful sample size, and highlights practical, flexible approaches to weight management, offering hope for long-term obesity control.
articles.personality_title: 12-week intermittent fasting helps people keep weight off for a year
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people struggle to keep weight off after losing it. Intermittent fasting, where you eat only during certain hours of the day, has become popular as a way to lose weight. But it was unclear if its effects last long after the fasting program ends.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Granada studied 99 adults who were overweight or obese. They followed a 12-week intermittent fasting program called 16:8, where participants ate only during an eight-hour window each day and fasted for 16 hours. Some ate early (9 a.m. to 5 p.m.), others late (1 p.m. to 9 p.m.), and a control group kept eating over 12 hours or more. One year after the program ended, those who fasted kept more weight off than the control group. The early eating group also kept more fat loss.

**Impact** – This study shows that just three months of intermittent fasting can help people maintain weight loss for at least a year. It also found that people can choose when to eat during the day, making fasting easier to fit into daily life. About one in three participants continued fasting on their own after the study, showing it can be a manageable habit.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope this flexible fasting method can be used more widely to help adults with overweight or obesity manage their weight. Future work may explore how intermittent fasting affects heart and bone health, and how it can be combined with other treatments for better results.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A 12-week intermittent fasting plan helped overweight adults keep weight off for a year, offering a simple and flexible way to support long-term weight management.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un jeûne intermittent de 12 semaines aide à maintenir la perte de poids pendant un an
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes ont du mal à conserver leur perte de poids. Le jeûne intermittent, qui consiste à manger seulement pendant certaines heures de la journée, est une méthode populaire pour perdre du poids. Mais on ne savait pas si ses effets duraient après la fin du programme.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Grenade ont étudié 99 adultes en surpoids ou obèses. Ils ont suivi un programme de jeûne intermittent de 12 semaines appelé 16:8, où les participants mangeaient seulement pendant une fenêtre de huit heures par jour et jeûnaient pendant 16 heures. Certains mangeaient tôt (de 9h à 17h), d'autres tard (de 13h à 21h), et un groupe témoin mangeait sur 12 heures ou plus. Un an après la fin du programme, ceux qui avaient jeûné avaient conservé plus de leur perte de poids que le groupe témoin. Le groupe mangeant tôt avait aussi gardé plus de perte de masse grasse.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre qu’un jeûne intermittent de seulement trois mois peut aider à garder la perte de poids pendant au moins un an. Elle a aussi montré que les gens peuvent choisir quand manger dans la journée, ce qui facilite l’intégration du jeûne dans la vie quotidienne. Environ un tiers des participants ont continué à jeûner seuls après l’étude, prouvant que c’est une habitude facile à adopter.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs espèrent que cette méthode flexible de jeûne pourra être utilisée plus largement pour aider les adultes en surpoids à gérer leur poids. Les recherches futures pourraient étudier comment le jeûne intermittent affecte la santé du cœur et des os, et comment l’associer à d’autres traitements.

**En une phrase** – Un programme de jeûne intermittent de 12 semaines a aidé des adultes en surpoids à maintenir leur perte de poids pendant un an, offrant une méthode simple et flexible pour gérer le poids sur le long terme.
articles.personality_title_es: El ayuno intermitente de 12 semanas ayuda a mantener la pérdida de peso durante un año
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas tienen dificultad para mantener el peso perdido. El ayuno intermitente, que consiste en comer solo durante ciertas horas del día, es una forma popular de bajar de peso. Pero no se sabía si sus efectos duran después de terminar el programa.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Granada estudiaron a 99 adultos con sobrepeso u obesidad. Siguieron un programa de ayuno intermitente de 12 semanas llamado 16:8, donde los participantes comían solo en una ventana de ocho horas al día y ayunaban 16 horas. Algunos comían temprano (de 9 a.m. a 5 p.m.), otros tarde (de 1 p.m. a 9 p.m.), y un grupo de control comía durante 12 horas o más. Un año después del programa, quienes hicieron ayuno mantuvieron más peso perdido que el grupo de control. El grupo que comía temprano también mantuvo más pérdida de grasa.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que solo tres meses de ayuno intermitente pueden ayudar a mantener la pérdida de peso por al menos un año. También encontró que las personas pueden elegir cuándo comer durante el día, lo que facilita incorporar el ayuno en su rutina diaria. Cerca de un tercio de los participantes continuaron ayunando por su cuenta después del estudio, demostrando que es un hábito manejable.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que este método flexible de ayuno pueda usarse más para ayudar a adultos con sobrepeso a controlar su peso. Futuras investigaciones podrían estudiar cómo el ayuno intermitente afecta la salud del corazón y los huesos, y cómo combinarlo con otros tratamientos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un plan de ayuno intermitente de 12 semanas ayudó a adultos con sobrepeso a mantener la pérdida de peso durante un año, ofreciendo una forma simple y flexible para apoyar el control del peso a largo plazo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a balanced scale holding a stylized eight-segment clock on one side and a simplified Mediterranean plate with fresh vegetables and olive oil on the other, set against a soft, natural background with gentle sunlight filtering through, symbolizing the harmony of intermittent fasting and healthy eating sustaining long-term weight management.

articles.title: The hidden cost of the night shift and how to sleep it off
articles.summary: More than three million people in the UK work night shifts, which can greatly impact their health. What can be done to help them?
articles.content:
To understand what shift work does to the body, it's worth looking at what emerging research suggests about sleep itself. Sleep does far more than give the brain and body a rest.

When we are asleep, our brain consolidates the memories of the day, processes emotions, and solves problems that defied it in the waking hours. It also strengthens immune defences and repairs muscle tissue.

Prof Russell Foster is a sleep scientist at Oxford University, who has spent a career studying the biology of the sleeping brain.

"Sleep is a pillar of our health," he says, "in the same way we think about diet and exercise. We have to take control of it."

In that light, the strain of shift work becomes easier to see: it's not solely about being tired, but potentially about repeatedly disrupting a system that's doing far more behind the scenes than many people realise.

One of the most remarkable discoveries of recent years is that while we sleep, the brain cleans itself. Deep within the grey matter is plumbing called the glymphatic system. Fluid runs along tiny channels beside the brain's blood vessels, washing away the waste products that accumulate during waking hours.

So, what happens to these toxins when sleep is disrupted?

Prof Hugh Markus, a neurologist who leads the stroke medicine group at the University of Cambridge, has begun to answer this question.

Markus and a medical student, Yutong Chen, analysed the brain scans of more than 40,000 people drawn from a vast database of health records and medical scans built up over more than a decade at the UK Biobank. All of them were healthy when their scans were taken.

The researchers found they could identify those whose drainage systems were struggling. Critically though, they discovered that those with the most impaired drainage systems were significantly more likely to go on to develop dementia years later, according to Markus.

"Disruption of that flow," he says, "was playing an important role in predicting who would get dementia, in large numbers of people in the normal population."
articles.reasoning: The article highlights important scientific research linking sleep disruption from night shift work to impaired brain waste clearance and increased dementia risk, revealing significant health implications for millions of workers. It provides meaningful context and expert insights, emphasizing sleep as a critical pillar of health and suggesting avenues for improving wellbeing among a large population.
articles.personality_title: New research reveals how night shifts affect brain health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – More than three million people in the UK work night shifts, which can change how their bodies and brains function. Scientists are studying how these changes affect overall health.

**What happened** – Researchers at Oxford and Cambridge universities studied sleep and its role in brain health. They found that during sleep, the brain cleans itself by removing waste through a system called the glymphatic system. Using brain scans from over 40,000 healthy people, scientists discovered that when this cleaning system is not working well, it can increase the chance of developing dementia later in life.

**Impact** – This research shows that sleep is not just rest but an important process that helps protect the brain. Night shift work can disrupt sleep and the brain’s cleaning system, which may explain why shift workers face higher health risks. Understanding this helps us see why good sleep is essential for long-term brain health.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to find ways to help night shift workers improve their sleep quality and protect their brain health. This could include new treatments or advice on managing sleep better to reduce the risks linked to shift work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Studies reveal that disrupted sleep from night shifts harms the brain’s cleaning system and raises dementia risk, highlighting the need to support shift workers’ sleep health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle recherche révèle l’impact des nuits de travail sur la santé du cerveau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plus de trois millions de personnes au Royaume-Uni travaillent de nuit, ce qui peut modifier le fonctionnement de leur corps et de leur cerveau. Les scientifiques étudient comment ces changements affectent la santé globale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs des universités d’Oxford et de Cambridge ont étudié le sommeil et son rôle dans la santé du cerveau. Ils ont découvert que pendant le sommeil, le cerveau se nettoie en éliminant les déchets grâce à un système appelé système glymphatique. En analysant les scanners cérébraux de plus de 40 000 personnes en bonne santé, ils ont constaté que lorsque ce système de nettoyage fonctionne mal, le risque de développer une démence plus tard augmente.

**Impact** – Cette recherche montre que le sommeil n’est pas seulement un repos, mais un processus important qui protège le cerveau. Le travail de nuit peut perturber le sommeil et le système de nettoyage du cerveau, ce qui pourrait expliquer pourquoi les travailleurs de nuit ont plus de risques pour leur santé. Comprendre cela montre pourquoi un bon sommeil est essentiel pour la santé cérébrale à long terme.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent trouver des moyens d’aider les travailleurs de nuit à améliorer la qualité de leur sommeil et à protéger leur cerveau. Cela pourrait inclure de nouveaux traitements ou des conseils pour mieux gérer le sommeil et réduire les risques liés au travail de nuit.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les études montrent que le sommeil perturbé par le travail de nuit endommage le système de nettoyage du cerveau et augmente le risque de démence, soulignant la nécessité de soutenir la santé du sommeil des travailleurs de nuit.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación revela cómo el trabajo nocturno afecta la salud cerebral
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Más de tres millones de personas en el Reino Unido trabajan en turnos nocturnos, lo que puede cambiar cómo funcionan sus cuerpos y cerebros. Los científicos están estudiando cómo estos cambios afectan la salud general.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de las universidades de Oxford y Cambridge estudiaron el sueño y su papel en la salud cerebral. Descubrieron que durante el sueño, el cerebro se limpia eliminando desechos a través de un sistema llamado sistema glinfático. Analizando escáneres cerebrales de más de 40,000 personas saludables, encontraron que cuando este sistema de limpieza no funciona bien, aumenta la probabilidad de desarrollar demencia en el futuro.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que el sueño no es solo descanso, sino un proceso importante que ayuda a proteger el cerebro. El trabajo nocturno puede interrumpir el sueño y el sistema de limpieza cerebral, lo que podría explicar por qué los trabajadores nocturnos tienen mayores riesgos para la salud. Entender esto ayuda a ver por qué un buen sueño es esencial para la salud cerebral a largo plazo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan encontrar formas de ayudar a los trabajadores nocturnos a mejorar la calidad de su sueño y proteger su salud cerebral. Esto podría incluir nuevos tratamientos o consejos para manejar mejor el sueño y reducir los riesgos relacionados con el trabajo nocturno.

**Frase clave** – Los estudios muestran que el sueño interrumpido por turnos nocturnos daña el sistema de limpieza del cerebro y aumenta el riesgo de demencia, resaltando la necesidad de apoyar la salud del sueño de los trabajadores nocturnos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene night scene inside a softly glowing human brain shaped like a cozy chamber, where gentle streams of clear fluid flow through intricate channels beside delicate blood vessels, symbolizing the glymphatic system cleansing away dark, shadowy particles representing toxins, with subtle hints of memory and emotion depicted as floating, luminous orbs being gently sorted and stored in peaceful compartments.
